image_prompt: SVG: automated external defibrillator (AED) unit with ECG screen and two adhesive pads applied to a patient.
SVG: two defibrillator types — Type 1 AED with stick-on adhesive pads, Type 2 handheld paddles with monitor.
SVG: a straw in a glass of water appearing bent at the water surface (refraction).
SVG: light ray travelling from air into water, bending toward the dashed normal at the boundary; Air and Water labelled.
SVG: observer on a cliff viewing a fish; real fish (solid) deeper, apparent fish (dashed) shallower, refracted light ray to the eye.
SVG: two eye diagrams — short-sighted eye focusing before the retina, and the same eye corrected by a diverging lens; labels Light rays, Retina, Diverging lens.
SVG: equipment list with icons — cotton reel, stick, rubber band, pencil.

SVG: motion DATA TABLE (Time / s vs Velocity / cm s-1). NOTE: the IB original here is a build-instructions photo, but the task text asks the student to read velocity at t=0.35 s, so this recreation is a data table (v=38 at 0.35 s) to make the task answerable. Please review this substitution.
SVG: velocity–time scatter graph (Velocity / cm s-1 0–50 vs Time / s 0–2.0), rise then fall.

render_data.title: Velocity–time graph of the cotton-reel car

readouts.note: start of line of best fit interval
end of line of best fit interval

render_data.gradientReadout: Gradient between t = 0.05 s and t = 0.35 s ≈ Δv/Δt ≈ 105 cm s⁻² (accept 100–110)
render_data.units: Gradient unit = cm s⁻²; the gradient represents acceleration
render_data.task: Calculate the gradient of the line of best fit between t = 0.05 s and t = 0.35 s, give its unit, and state the quantity it represents.

image_prompt: SVG: schematic of the cotton-reel model car (reel, wound elastic band, stick lever, pencil axle). NOTE: the IB original here is essentially repeated text, not a diagram — this recreation gives a useful labelled figure for the design task. Please review whether an image is wanted here at all.
SVG: thermoelectric generator bridging a hot-water tank and a cold-water tank, with electrical wires; labels Hot water, Cold water, Water tanks, Thermoelectric generator, Electrical wires.

SVG: data table Temperature 1/°C, Temperature 2/°C, Temperature difference/°C, Voltage/V. Four rows from the original plus a highlighted blank-difference row for the student to fill in. NOTE: the 5th row's other values are illustrative (cut off in the IB crop) — review.

render_data.title: Thermoelectric generator (TEG): voltage vs temperature difference
render_data.task: One temperature difference is missing from the table: ΔT = T₁ − T₂ = 20.0 °C. Plot V against ΔT, judge whether voltage is directly proportional to ΔT, and find the Seebeck coefficient from the gradient (S = −ΔV/ΔT).

image_prompt: SVG: Newton's cannonball thought experiment — globe with mountain V, projectile paths of increasing range and orbital circles, labelled A–G and V.

render_data.title: Newton's cannonball — circular orbit around Earth
render_data.caption: Fired horizontally from a tall mountain, the cannonball's path depends on its initial horizontal speed: 0 m s⁻¹ drops straight down; 4700 m s⁻¹ curves back to the surface; 7800 m s⁻¹ gives a circular orbit; 14 200 m s⁻¹ is fast enough to escape Earth's gravity. The only force acting is gravity, directed toward Earth's centre.

image_prompt: SVG: four Earth diagrams A–D, each with a cannon on top firing at increasing speed — short arc, partial orbit, circular orbit, escape ellipse. 'Diagram not to scale'.

render_data.title: Matching cannonball launch speeds to their paths (A–D)
render_data.caption: Correct matches (all four required): C = 0 m s⁻¹ (falls straight down); A = 4700 m s⁻¹ (curved arc that falls back to Earth); B = 7800 m s⁻¹ (circular orbit); D = 14 200 m s⁻¹ (escape trajectory). Animation shows the B = 7800 m s⁻¹ circular-orbit case with gravity directed toward Earth's centre.

image_prompt: SVG: satellite (dot) in a dashed circular orbit around Earth with direction arrows.

render_data.title: Cannonball / satellite in a circular orbit around Earth
render_data.caption: In a circular orbit the only force on the cannonball is gravity (its weight), which always points toward the centre of the Earth and provides the centripetal force. The correct free-body diagram is B — a single arrow directed inward toward Earth's centre, with no thrust.

image_prompt: SVG: four free-body diagrams A–D of the orbiting satellite — A Thrust up + Weight right, B Weight right, C Weight up, D Thrust up.
